papers.title: An Efficient Gradient-Based Inference Attack for Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Federated Learning is a machine learning setting that reduces direct data exposure, improving the privacy guarantees of machine learning models. Yet, the exchange of model updates between the participants and the aggregator can still leak sensitive information. In this work, we present a new gradient-based membership inference attack for federated learning scenarios that exploits the temporal evolution of last-layer gradients across multiple federated rounds. Our method uses the shadow technique to learn round-wise gradient patterns of the training records, requiring no access to the private dataset, and is designed to consider both semi-honest and malicious adversaries (aggregators or data owners). Beyond membership inference, we also provide a natural extension of the proposed attack to discrete attribute inference by contrasting gradient responses under alternative attribute hypotheses. The proposed attacks are model-agnostic, and therefore applicable to any gradient-based model and can be applied to both classification and regression settings. We evaluate the attack on CIFAR-100 and Purchase100 datasets for membership inference and on Breast Cancer Wisconsin for attribute inference. Our findings reveal strong attack performance and comparable computational and memory overhead in membership inference when compared to another attack from the literature. The obtained results emphasize that multi-round federated learning can increase the vulnerability to inference attacks, that aggregators pose a more substantial threat than data owners, and that attack performance is strongly influenced by the nature of the training dataset, with richer, high-dimensional data leading to stronger leakage than simpler tabular data.

This aligns with the user's idea of exploiting gradient update history from a compromised participant to reconstruct membership. However, the paper's method specifically focuses on the 'norm of the last-layer gradients' and uses a 'binary logistic regression classifier' with a 'shadow technique,' which introduces meaningful differences in the specific design and components compared to a general 'gradient update history' exploitation. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on membership inference attacks specifically tailored to federated learning. The paper's abstract explicitly states, 'we present a new gradient-based membership inference attack for federated learning scenarios that exploits the temporal evolution of last-layer gradients across multiple federated rounds,' which directly aligns with the user's idea of 'exploits the gradient update history from a compromised participant, reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch.' | Contribution: The paper's core contribution is a 'new gradient-based membership inference attack for federated learning scenarios that exploits the temporal evolution of last-layer gradients across multiple federated rounds,' which directly matches the user's idea of 'A membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits the gradient update history from a compromised participant, reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch.' Both focus on gradient history in FL for membership inference.

papers.title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
papers.abstract:
Deep neural networks are susceptible to various inference attacks as they remember information about their training data. We design white-box inference attacks to perform a comprehensive privacy analysis of deep learning models. We measure the privacy leakage through parameters of fully trained models as well as the parameter updates of models during training. We design inference algorithms for both centralized and federated learning, with respect to passive and active inference attackers, and assuming different adversary prior knowledge.
  We evaluate our novel white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records. We show that a straightforward extension of the known black-box attacks to the white-box setting (through analyzing the outputs of activation functions) is ineffective. We therefore design new algorithms tailored to the white-box setting by exploiting the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm, which is the algorithm used to train deep neural networks. We investigate the reasons why deep learning models may leak information about their training data. We then show that even well-generalized models are significantly susceptible to white-box membership inference attacks, by analyzing state-of-the-art pre-trained and publicly available models for the CIFAR dataset. We also show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants, even when the global model achieves high prediction accuracies.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea. It explicitly states, 'We design white-box inference algorithms for both centralized and federated learning... We evaluate our novel white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records.' Furthermore, it details how 'adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants,' and specifically mentions exploiting 'the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm' and using 'the gradient vector of the model... as the main feature for the attack,' which aligns perfectly with exploiting gradient update history to reconstruct sample usage. | Method: The paper proposes a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient updates, specifically mentioning 'repeated parameter updates of the models over different epochs on the same underlying training set is a key factor in boosting the inference attack accuracy' and designing 'active attack that performs gradient ascent on a set of target data points before uploading and updating the global parameters'. This directly aligns with the user's idea of exploiting 'gradient update history from a compromised participant' to reconstruct 'whether a specific sample was used'. | Domain: The user's research idea explicitly describes a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history. The paper directly addresses this by designing 'white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records' in both 'centralized and federated learning' settings, specifically exploiting 'the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm' and noting that 'the repeated parameter updates of the models over different epochs on the same underlying training set is a key factor in boosting the inference attack accuracy' in federated learning. | Contribution: The paper directly claims and demonstrates a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits parameter updates (which include gradient information) from participants. It explicitly states, 'We design inference algorithms for both centralized and federated learning... We evaluate our novel white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records.' and 'In federated learning, we show that a curious parameter server or even a participant can perform alarmingly accurate membership inference attacks against other participants.' The paper also details 'active membership inference attacks against other participants' where an 'adversarial participant can actively push SGD to leak even more information about the participants' data' by manipulating gradients, directly matching the user's idea of exploiting gradient update history from a compromised participant to reconstruct if a sample was used in a local training batch.

papers.title: A Reputation Mechanism Is All You Need: Collaborative Fairness and Adversarial Robustness in Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging practical framework for effective and scalable machine learning among multiple participants, such as end users, organizations and companies. However, most existing FL or distributed learning frameworks have not well addressed two important issues together: collaborative fairness and adversarial robustness (e.g. free-riders and malicious participants). In conventional FL, all participants receive the global model (equal rewards), which might be unfair to the high-contributing participants. Furthermore, due to the lack of a safeguard mechanism, free-riders or malicious adversaries could game the system to access the global model for free or to sabotage it. In this paper, we propose a novel Robust and Fair Federated Learning (RFFL) framework to achieve collaborative fairness and adversarial robustness simultaneously via a reputation mechanism. RFFL maintains a reputation for each participant by examining their contributions via their uploaded gradients (using vector similarity) and thus identifies non-contributing or malicious participants to be removed. Our approach differentiates itself by not requiring any auxiliary/validation dataset. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that RFFL can achieve high fairness and is very robust to different types of adversaries while achieving competitive predictive accuracy.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on a 'membership inference attack tailored to federated learning' to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in training. In contrast, the paper addresses 'collaborative fairness and adversarial robustness' in federated learning, specifically focusing on identifying and mitigating 'free-riders and malicious participants' through a reputation mechanism. While both involve analyzing gradients in FL, the core problem of inferring membership of a specific sample is distinct from identifying malicious participants or free-riders. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a membership inference attack that reconstructs whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch by exploiting gradient update history. In contrast, the paper proposes a Robust and Fair Federated Learning (RFFL) framework that uses a reputation mechanism to identify non-contributing or malicious participants based on the similarity of their uploaded gradients to the aggregated global gradients. The methods are fundamentally different: one is an attack, the other is a defense/fairness mechanism, with no overlap in their specific techniques or goals. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper operate within the domain of federated learning and focus on adversarial attacks. The user's idea specifically targets membership inference attacks, while the paper addresses 'adversarial robustness' against 'targeted poisoning, untargeted poisoning and free-riders' by examining 'uploaded gradients'. This indicates a shared sub-area of federated learning security, with closely related application targets concerning gradient-based attacks. | Contribution: The user's research idea focuses on a membership inference attack to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch by exploiting gradient update history. In contrast, the paper proposes RFFL, a framework to achieve collaborative fairness and adversarial robustness by using a reputation mechanism to identify non-contributing or malicious participants (e.g., free-riders, targeted poisoning, untargeted poisoning) based on gradient similarity. The contributions address different problems and propose different mechanisms.

papers.title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
papers.abstract:
Deep neural networks are susceptible to various inference attacks as they remember information about their training data. We design white-box inference attacks to perform a comprehensive privacy analysis of deep learning models. We measure the privacy leakage through parameters of fully trained models as well as the parameter updates of models during training. We design inference algorithms for both centralized and federated learning, with respect to passive and active inference attackers, and assuming different adversary prior knowledge.
  We evaluate our novel white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records. We show that a straightforward extension of the known black-box attacks to the white-box setting (through analyzing the outputs of activation functions) is ineffective. We therefore design new algorithms tailored to the white-box setting by exploiting the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm, which is the algorithm used to train deep neural networks. We investigate the reasons why deep learning models may leak information about their training data. We then show that even well-generalized models are significantly susceptible to white-box membership inference attacks, by analyzing state-of-the-art pre-trained and publicly available models for the CIFAR dataset. We also show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants, even when the global model achieves high prediction accuracies.

papers.title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging distributed machine learning framework for collaborative model training with a network of clients (edge devices). FL offers default client privacy by allowing clients to keep their sensitive data on local devices and to only share local training parameter updates with the federated server. However, recent studies have shown that even sharing local parameter updates from a client to the federated server may be susceptible to gradient leakage attacks and intrude the client privacy regarding its training data. In this paper, we present a principled framework for evaluating and comparing different forms of client privacy leakage attacks. We first provide formal and experimental analysis to show how adversaries can reconstruct the private local training data by simply analyzing the shared parameter update from local training (e.g., local gradient or weight update vector). We then analyze how different hyperparameter configurations in federated learning and different settings of the attack algorithm may impact on both attack effectiveness and attack cost. Our framework also measures, evaluates, and analyzes the effectiveness of client privacy leakage attacks under different gradient compression ratios when using communication efficient FL protocols. Our experiments also include some preliminary mitigation strategies to highlight the importance of providing a systematic attack evaluation framework towards an in-depth understanding of the various forms of client privacy leakage threats in federated learning and developing theoretical foundations for attack mitigation.

While membership inference is mentioned in the 'Related work' section as a type of privacy exploit, the paper's core problem is a more general data reconstruction attack, not specifically membership inference. | Method: The user's idea focuses on a 'membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits the gradient update history'. The user's idea specifies a 'membership inference attack tailored to federated learning,' while the paper discusses 'gradient leakage attacks' and mentions 'membership inference' as a related privacy exploit. existing_examples.name: Membership Inference Attacks on Federated Learning
existing_examples.description: Numerous research papers have explored membership inference attacks (MIA) specifically in the context of federated learning (FL). These attacks aim to determine if a specific data record was part of a client's local training dataset. Many of these leverage gradient information.

existing_examples.name: Gradient-based Membership Inference Attacks
existing_examples.description: General membership inference attacks often rely on analyzing model outputs or gradients. The idea of exploiting gradient updates to infer membership is a well-established technique in the broader MIA literature, which has been adapted to FL.

existing_examples.name: Specific Research Papers (e.g., 'Membership Inference Attacks Against Federated Learning' by Nasr et al., 2019; 'Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Federated Learning' by Shokri et al., 2017 - though not FL specific, it laid groundwork for gradient-based MIA)
existing_examples.description: Several seminal and subsequent works have demonstrated and refined membership inference attacks against federated learning, often by analyzing shared gradients or model updates. The concept of a 'compromised participant' (e.g., a malicious server or another client colluding) is also a common threat model.

reality_check.assessment: The idea of a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history from a compromised participant to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch is a well-known and extensively researched area. It has been demonstrated in numerous academic papers and is considered a significant privacy vulnerability in federated learning. The core mechanism described (exploiting gradient history from a compromised entity) is a standard approach within this field.
novelty_aspects: Specific novel techniques for gradient analysis (e.g., new statistical tests, machine learning models for inference)
Exploiting a very specific type of 'compromised participant' or attack vector not previously considered

Demonstrating the attack's effectiveness under new, highly constrained, or previously unstudied federated learning settings (e.g., extreme data heterogeneity, very high noise, specific aggregation algorithms)
reality_check.recommendation: While the general concept is not novel, there is still room for research in developing *more effective*, *more efficient*, or *more stealthy* membership inference attacks under specific, challenging federated learning scenarios, or in developing *novel defenses* against such attacks. If pursuing this, clearly articulate what specific, new technical contribution or insight your approach offers beyond existing literature.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: In a membership inference attack, however, the attacker's objective is to infer if a particular individual data record was included in the training dataset [4], [5], [6]. matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: We design white-box inference attacks that exploit the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithm . Each data point in the training set influences many of the model parameters, through the SGD algorithm, to minimize its contribution to the model's training loss. The local gradient of the loss on a target data record, with respect to a given parameter, indicates how much and in which direction the parameter needs to be changed to fit the model to the data record. Therefore, each training data sample will leave a distinguishable footprint on the gradients of the loss function over the model's parameters .
matched_sections.reason: The paper describes exploiting 'privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithm' and that 'each training data sample will leave a distinguishable footprint on the gradients of the loss function over the model's parameters', which is very similar to the user's method of exploiting 'gradient update history'.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Our contributions. In this paper, we present a comprehensive framework for the privacy analysis of deep neural networks, using white-box membership inference attacks. We go beyond membership inference attacks against fully-trained models. We take all major scenarios where deep learning is used for training and fine-tuning or updating models, with one or multiple collaborative data holders, when attacker only passively observes the model updates or actively influences the target model in order to extract more information, and for attackers with different types of prior knowledge.
matched_sections.reason: The paper outlines its contribution as a comprehensive framework for white-box membership inference attacks, considering scenarios with multiple collaborative data holders (federated learning) and attackers observing model updates or actively influencing the model. This is very similar to the user's proposed contribution.

We demonstrated our attacks in the stand-alone and federated settings, with respect to passive and active inference attackers, and assuming different adversary prior knowledge.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: RELEVANT SECTIONS
matched_sections.text_snippet: In federated learning, we show that a curious parameter server or even a participant can perform alarmingly accurate membership inference attacks against other participants. For the DenseNet model on CIFAR100, a local participant can achieve a membership inference accuracy of 72 . 2% , even though it only observes aggregate updates through the parameter server. Also, the curious central parameter server can achieve a 79 . 2% inference accuracy, as it receives the individual parameter updates from all participants.
matched_sections.reason: This section explicitly states that the paper shows a participant can perform accurate membership inference attacks against other participants in federated learning, which is a direct demonstration of the user's proposed contribution.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Reputation Mechanism Is All You Need: Collaborative Fairness and Adversarial Robustness in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging practical framework for effective and scalable machine learning among multiple participants, such as end users, organizations and companies. However, most existing FL or distributed learning frameworks have not well addressed two important issues together: collaborative fairness and adversarial robustness (e.g. free-riders and malicious participants).
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address challenges within federated learning. The user's idea focuses on a specific type of attack (membership inference), which falls under the broader category of 'adversarial robustness' that the paper aims to address, although the paper's specific problems are free-riders and malicious participants, not membership inference.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Reputation Mechanism Is All You Need: Collaborative Fairness and Adversarial Robustness in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Federated learning (FL) (McMahan et al., 2017) provides a promising collaboration paradigm by enabling a multitude of participants to construct a joint model without exposing their private training data. Two emerging challenges in FL are collaborative fairness (participants with different contributions should be rewarded differently), and adversarial robustness (free-riders should not enjoy the global model for free, and malicious participants should not compromise system integrity) (Lyu et al., 2020c).
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea identifies a specific attack (membership inference) within federated learning, which aligns with the paper's general concern for 'adversarial robustness' in FL, even if the specific attack types differ.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Reputation Mechanism Is All You Need: Collaborative Fairness and Adversarial Robustness in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: In this paper, we propose a novel Robust and Fair Federated Learning (RFFL) framework to achieve collaborative fairness and adversarial robustness simultaneously via a reputation mechanism. RFFL maintains a reputation for each participant by examining their contributions via their uploaded gradients (using vector similarity) and thus identifies non-contributing or malicious participants to be removed.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea proposes an attack, while the paper proposes a defense mechanism. While both relate to security in federated learning and involve analyzing gradients, their contributions are opposite in nature (attack vs. defense). The paper's contribution is a framework for robustness, whereas the user's is an attack method.

RFFL utilizes a reputation system to iteratively calculate participants' contributions and reward participants accordingly with different models of performance commensurate with their contributions.

matched_sections.heading: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Several recent research efforts have shown that the default privacy in FL is insufficient for protecting the underlaying training data from privacy leakage attacks by gradient-based reconstruction [10,45,43]. By intercepting the local gradient update shared by a client to the FL server before performing federated averaging [28,25,17], the adversary can reconstruct the local training data with high reconstruction accuracy, and hence intrudes the client privacy and deceives the FL system by sneaking into client confidential training data illegally and silently, making the FL system vulnerable to client privacy leakage attacks.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states that adversaries can reconstruct local training data by intercepting local gradient updates, which aligns with the user's problem of reconstructing whether a specific sample was used.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: The Client Privacy Leakage (CPL) Attack: An Overview
matched_sections.text_snippet: The client privacy leakage attack is a gradient-based feature reconstruction attack, in which the attacker can design a gradient-based reconstruction learning algorithm that will take the gradient update at round t , say ∇ w k ( t ), to be shared by the client, to reconstruct the private data used in the local training computation.
matched_sections.reason: The paper describes a 'gradient-based feature reconstruction attack' that takes the gradient update to reconstruct private data, which is very similar to the user's proposed method of exploiting 'gradient update history' to reconstruct whether a sample was used.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning

matched_sections.text_snippet: We first provide formal and experimental analysis to show how adversaries can reconstruct the private local training data by simply analyzing the shared parameter update from local training (e.g., local gradient or weight update vector).
matched_sections.reason: The paper's method involves reconstructing private local training data by analyzing shared parameter updates (local gradients), which directly corresponds to the user's method of exploiting gradient update history to reconstruct sample usage.

FL offers default client privacy by allowing clients to keep their sensitive data on local devices and to only share local training parameter updates with the federated server.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: However, recent studies have shown that even sharing local parameter updates from a client to the federated server may be susceptible to gradient leakage attacks and intrude the client privacy regarding its training data. In this paper, we present a principled framework for evaluating and comparing different forms of client privacy leakage attacks. We first provide formal and experimental analysis to show how adversaries can reconstruct the private local training data by simply analyzing the shared parameter update from local training (e.g., local gradient or weight update vector).
matched_sections.reason: The paper's contribution is a framework for evaluating gradient leakage attacks and showing how adversaries can reconstruct private data from gradient updates. The user's idea proposes a specific membership inference attack that exploits gradient history for sample reconstruction. There is overlap in the goal of reconstructing private data from gradients, but the user's idea is a specific attack, whereas the paper is a framework for evaluation.
